confidence.disclosure: Abu al-Qasim al-Zahrawi, the great surgeon of al-Andalus known to Latin Europe as Abulcasis, lived almost his entire life in Madinat al-Zahra and Cordoba. His biography is poorly documented in the surviving Andalusian sources (Ibn Juljul mentions him only briefly), and most of what is known comes from the autobiographical hints in his medical encyclopaedia al-Tasrif. The journey is therefore almost stationary - just two cities - but the dates are reasonably firm: born in or near Madinat al-Zahra in 936, lived through the height of Cordoba and the beginning of its collapse, and died around 1013.

sources: Al-Tasrif li-man 'ajiza 'an al-ta'lif (Al-Zahrawi's own work, especially the introduction)
Ibn Abi Usaybi'a, 'Uyun al-Anba fi Tabaqat al-Atibba
Sami Hamarneh, The Life Sciences of al-Zahrawi (1961)

journey.event: Born around 936 in or near Madinat al-Zahra, the new royal city built by the Umayyad caliph Abd al-Rahman III about 8 km west of Cordoba. The exact place is debated; the nisba al-Zahrawi suggests he was born in or closely associated with this city. His family is of Arab origin from the Ansar tribe of Medina.

Grows up in the orbit of the Umayyad court of Cordoba, then at the height of its power and wealth. The library of al-Hakam II, said to contain 400,000 volumes, gives him access to the Greek and Arabic medical tradition - Hippocrates, Galen, Paul of Aegina, al-Razi, and many others.

Trains as a physician in Cordoba. Studies the works of the Greek and Arabic medical authorities and begins his own clinical practice. The teacher of his teachers is unknown but he draws heavily on the surgical chapters of Paul of Aegina's Epitome and on the Hawi of al-Razi.

Becomes one of the principal court physicians of the Umayyad caliph al-Hakam II (reigned 961-976). Practices in Cordoba and Madinat al-Zahra, treating both the royal household and the people of the city. His clinical experience over decades will become the basis of his great encyclopaedia.

Begins composing his great medical encyclopaedia al-Tasrif li-man 'ajiza 'an al-ta'lif (The Arrangement for Him Who Cannot Compile a Book of His Own), in 30 treatises covering the whole of medicine. He works on it for many years, drawing on his own clinical experience as well as the earlier Greek and Arabic literature.

Completes the al-Tasrif. Its 30th and most famous treatise is the surgical book, the first systematic and illustrated treatise on surgery in any language. Al-Zahrawi describes about 200 surgical instruments (many of his own design), procedures including amputation, lithotomy, cauterisation, the treatment of fractures and dislocations, and the management of obstructed labour. He emphasises that surgery requires both anatomical knowledge and long clinical training.

Lives through the beginning of the fitna of al-Andalus, the civil war that destroys the Umayyad caliphate. In 1009-1010 Madinat al-Zahra is sacked by Berber troops; the great library and palaces are looted and burned. Al-Zahrawi witnesses the destruction of the city in which he was born.

Dies around 1013 (404 AH) in Cordoba, aged about 77. The exact circumstances are not preserved. Within decades his al-Tasrif is translated into Latin (the surgical book by Gerard of Cremona in the 12th century), and as 'Albucasis' he becomes the principal authority on surgery in medieval European universities, a position he holds until the 16th century.
journey.source: Ibn Abi Usaybi'a; Encyclopaedia of Islam
